coredata.dc:title: Sulfhydryl-functionalized carbon dots as effective probes for fluorescence enhancement detection of patulin
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Kunfeng
Guo, Yahui
Yu, Hang
Cheng, Yuliang
Xie, Yunfei
Yao, Weirong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, sulfydryl-functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SH-NCDs) was synthesized by amide reaction of hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots with l-cysteine and used to detect patulin selectively. The SH-NCDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence in the range 300–360 nm. The modified sulfhydryl group (-SH) on the surface of NCDs served as a specific recognition site to capture patulin. The addition reaction between patulin and the -SH on the SH-NCDs surface resulted in enhanced fluorescence. SH-NCDs was used as a fluorescent probe for label-free detection of patulin, showing excellent sensitivity in the linear range of 0.1–400 ng mL−1, with detection limits as low as 0.053 ng mL−1. The fluorescent probe has specific selectivity for patulin. The recoveries of patulin in apple juice and grape juice were 88.9 %–99.2 % and 92.5 %–101.8 %, respectively. These results showed that the sensor designed in this experiment selectively detected the target patulin from complex food systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SH-NCDs
Fluorescence sensors
Patulin
Detection
Fruit juice

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of SH-NCDs
The stability of photoluminescence (PL) in SH-NCDs
Establishment of a patulin detection method with SH-NCDs
Optimization of experimental conditions on the reaction of patulin with SH-NCDs
Evaluation of the patulin detection method with SH-NCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work described in this article was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32172326), the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFC1606800), the Modern agriculture in Jiangsu Province - key and general projects (BE2019362), the Science and technology planning project of Jiangsu market supervision and Administration Bureau (KJ204132, KJ21125006), the Open Project of Engineering Research Center of Dairy Quality and Safety Control Technology of the Ministry of Education of China (R202101), the Suzhou science and technology development plan (SS2019016), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680064), and the Postdoctoral Research Startup Fee of Jiangnan University (1025219032200190).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of the formation process of SH-NCDs and their application for the detection of patulin. (B) Transmission electron microscopy image of the NCDs and (D) SH-NCDs, (C) the size distribution of NCDs, and (E) SH-NCDs.

(A) FT-IR curves of (a) citric acid, (b) NCDs, (c) l-cysteine, (d) SH-NCDs, and (e) SH-NCDs + PAT. (B) The full range XPS spectra of NCDs and SH-NCDs. (C) The high resolution S 2p XPS spectra of SH-NCDs. (D–F) High-resolution XPS s pectra of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of the NCDs, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption of NCDs, SH-NCDs, and SH-NCDs + PAT. (B) PL spectra of NCDs and (C) SH-NCDs. (D) The fluorescence maximum excitation and emission spectrum of NCDs and SH-NCDs. (E) PL spectrum changes of NCDs and SH-NCDs before and after the addition of patulin. (F) NCDs in aqueous solution under the illumination of sunlight (left) and UV light (365 nm, right). (G) Effect of irradiation time at 307 nm (H) Emission intensity of SH-NCDs in solutions of different pH. (I) Emission intensity of SH-NCDs in different concentrations of NaCl solution.

(A) Effect of pH and (B) time on the reaction of patulin with SH-NCDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the SH-NCDs in response to PAT at various concentrations and (D) their corresponding calibration curves. (E) Specificity was studied toward the blank, OTA, ZEN, AFB1, DON, PAT, and their mixtures. The concentrations of PAT and other mycotoxins were 50 ng mL−1 and 200 ng mL−1, respectively. (F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of mycotoxin, respectively.)
Determination of patulin in apple juice and grape juice (n = 3).
Comparison of patulin detection performance based on sensor.

head.ce:title: Sulfhydryl-functionalized carbon dots as effective probes for fluorescence enhancement detection of patulin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University, No. 1800, Lihu Road, Binhu Avenue, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, sulfydryl-functionalized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (SH-NCDs) was synthesized by amide reaction of hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots with l-cysteine and used to detect patulin selectively. The SH-NCDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence in the range 300–360 nm. The modified sulfhydryl group (-SH) on the surface of NCDs served as a specific recognition site to capture patulin. The addition reaction between patulin and the -SH on the SH-NCDs surface resulted in enhanced fluorescence. SH-NCDs was used as a fluorescent probe for label-free detection of patulin, showing excellent sensitivity in the linear range of 0.1–400 ng mL−1, with detection limits as low as 0.053 ng mL−1. The fluorescent probe has specific selectivity for patulin. The recoveries of patulin in apple juice and grape juice were 88.9 %–99.2 % and 92.5 %–101.8 %, respectively. These results showed that the sensor designed in this experiment selectively detected the target patulin from complex food systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is closely related to people's lives, and contamination of food by mycotoxins poses a certain risk to human health. Patulin, a highly toxic mycotoxin metabolite produced by Penicillium, Byssochlamys, and Aspergillus, is usually found in fruits, vegetables, and cereals, especially in rotten fruits and concentrated fruit juices (Hatamluyi et al., 2020; Solairaj et al., 2020). Studies have shown that exposure to patulin may cause a certain toxic effect on the human body, including neurotoxicity, immunotoxicity, teratogenicity, and carcinogenicity. It may also cause certain damage to the lungs, the liver, gastrointestinal system, and kidneys (He & Lu, 2020; Wei et al., 2020). Due to the high toxicity of patulin and its widespread presence in foods, many countries have set limits on its residues. For example, the Food and Drug Administration of the United States and China stipulates that the maximum residue of patulin in fruit juice and fruit processing products is 50 ng g−1, and the limit standard set by the European Union in infants and children food is 10 ng g−1 (Baoshan He & Dong, 2021). Because of the widespread presence and high toxicity of patulin in food, it is necessary to develop a rapid and sensitive detection method to avoid the potential harm of patulin to human health. Traditional detection and quantification methods for patulin mainly include thin-layer chromatography (Welke et al., 2009), high-performance liquid chromatography (Prieta et al., 1993), gas chromatography (Marsol-Vall et al., 2016), high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (Li et al., 2017), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Kharandi et al., 2013), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Wu et al., 2016). Although these methods have already been proven to possess excellent reproducibility and good sensitivity, most of the instruments are expensive, complex in sample preparation, and require specialized operators, which greatly limits their widespread use (Wu et al., 2018). Recently, numerous detection methods, including electrochemical (Selvam et al., 2021), optical (Yan et al., 2022), and colorimetric methods (Wu et al., 2016), have been developed to address these problems faced by traditional methods. For example, He et al. used PEI-rGO/Fe-MOFs/Pt@AuNRs as an electrode-modification material to develop a Zr MOFs-labeled electrochemical aptamer sensor for the detection of patulin with a limit of detection of 0.04 ng L−1 in a linear range of 0.05–500 ng L−1 (He and Dong, 2021). Antibodies-based immuno-electrochemical sensors have been established to detect patulin and could achieve rapid detection of patulin but have poor selectivity and are susceptible to Bovine Serum Albumin interference (Song et al., 2021). Although these electrochemical methods have high specificity and sensitivity, their deficiencies are also conspicuous. First, antibodies and aptamers have problems such as high preparation costs and complicated operations. Second, these electrochemical sensors require complicated electrode pretreatment and strict immobilization. Therefore, it is necessary to explore a new method to compensate for these deficiencies and achieve faster and more sensitive detection of patulin.

In recent years, fluorescence analysis technology has attracted more and more attention due to the advantages of simple and convenient instruments, low cost, and high sensitivity. In fluorescence analysis, fluorescent probes play a crucial role. The commonly used fluorescent probes include nanomaterial and organic small molecule, such as carbon dots (CDs), metal nanoclusters, metal nanoparticles, upconversion nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), and organic dye molecules. A perfect fluorescent probe must have good luminescence properties, high water solubility, strong photostability, and biocompatibility. Compared with metal nanoparticles, QDs, and organic dye molecules, CDs show considerable potential in fluorescence analysis. They are less than 10 nm in size, low toxicity, easy to prepare, and have variable fluorescence. In addition, they also have excellent chemical inertness, high water solubility, and good biocompatibility (Speranskaya et al., 2020). Therefore, the application of CDs in fluorescence sensors has attracted more and more attention. The functional groups on the surface of CDs not only strongly affect their optical properties but also have key recognition regions. Because of the lack of a variety of active sites on the surface for pristine CDs, the detection process is often subject to potential interference, limiting its application. Therefore, it is necessary to modify CDs to improve their optical properties and broaden the application range of CDs.

Patulin has good water solubility, is stable under acidic conditions, and easily decomposed under alkaline conditions. It is an unsaturated hybrid lactone, prone to react with sulfhydryl-containing compounds (Glaser & Stopper, 2012). Inspired by these characteristics, we established a novel fluorescence sensor with SH-NCDs as a probe for the detection of patulin. SH-NCDs was synthesized from hydrothermally synthesized NCDs and l-cysteine through an amide reaction. The surface morphology and chemical structure were tested by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. A diagram of the fabrication of SH-NCDs and detection of patulin are shown in Fig. 1 A. The detection method of patulin was established after optimizing the detection conditions, and the sensitivity and specification were evaluated in juice, which will realize in the application of SH-NCDs in patulin detection.

Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), ochratoxin A (OTA), deoxynivalenol (DON), and patulin (PAT) were bought from Tianjin Alta Technology Co., ltd (China). Citric acid, l-cysteine, and ammonia solution (25 %) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (China). 1-Ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbon diimine hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). The dialysis cassette (1,000 Da) was from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (China).

The fluorescence spectra of SH-NCDs were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan). Morphology measurements of NCDs and SH-NCDs were performed with a FEI F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The chemical composition of NCDs and SH-NCDs was analyzed using Kα XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded by a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing General Co., ltd, China). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured with a Nicolet iS5 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA).

The preparation of NCDs was employed according to Zhang’s method (Zhang et al., 2016) with slight modifications. The steps were as follows: 4.2 g of citric acid and 4.5 mL of aqueous ammonia were added to 40 mL of deionized water and stirred to dissolve. The mixed solution was sealed in an autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was complete, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain a dark green product and then a 0.22 μm filter was used to remove larger particles. The filtrate was then dialyzed for 24 h using a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted substances and small molecules. Finally, the purified NCDs solution was dried in a vacuum oven (60 ℃) to obtain solid NCDs. The solid NCDs was then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

NCDs (5.0 mg) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and activated by EDC (0.5 g) and NHS (0.6 g) for 40 min. Then, 0.6 g of l-cysteine was added to the mixed solution, the pH of the mixture solution was adjusted to 7 with sodium hydroxide, and the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the mixture was dialyzed for 24 h to obtain SH-NCDs.

The process of detecting patulin with the prepared fluorescence sensor was as follows: 200 μL SH-NCDs and 100 μL patulin of different concentrations were added to a centrifuge tube in sequence, and then the mixed solution was made up to 1 mL with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mmol L−1, pH 7.0). After incubation at room temperature for 15 min, the emission spectrum of the mixed solution at 419 nm was measured.

The fluorescence sensor prepared in this experiment was used to detect patulin in apple juice and grape juice to evaluate the practicability of this method. The apple juice and grape juice used in the experiment were purchased from nearby supermarkets. Samples were pretreated according to the sample pretreatment method used by Wu et al. (2018). First, 1 mL of the juice sample was added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube, then 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added, mixed by shaking and centrifuged (1665g, 5 min). After the centrifugation, the supernatant was removed and dried by nitrogen blowing. The solid obtained after nitrogen blowing was reconstituted in PBS and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter for subsequent experiments. After testing, the juice sample did not contain patulin, and then a spiking experiment was performed, and three spike concentrations (1, 10, and 50 ng mL−1) were taken for recovery experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of SH-NCDs

ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and microstructure of NCDs and SH-NCDs were characterized using TEM techniques. Fig. 1B and 1D show the TEM images of NCDs and SH-NCDs particles, respectively, which were uniformly distributed without apparent aggregation. Fig. 1C shows that the average diameter of NCDs was 1.88 ± 0.36 nm, whereas that of SH-NCDs was 2.46 ± 0.44 nm (Fig. 1E).

The functional group composition of SH-NCDs was investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of NCDs, SH-NCDs, l-cysteine, and citric acid are shown in Fig. 2 A. By comparing the FT-IR spectra of citric acid and NCDs, we could see that most of the characteristic sharp peaks of citric acid disappeared, indicating citric acid was thermally decomposed during the formation of NCDs. The new band at 1567 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CN, confirming that they were N-doped CDs (Kumar et al., 2014). SH-NCDs had a characteristic peak at 2550 cm−1 that was not found in NCDs, which belonged to the SH stretching vibration, whereas it could be seen in l-cysteine. At the same time, the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl and amide groups in NCDs and SH-NCDs gradually shifted from 1712 to 1644 cm−1. This result fully proved that l-cysteine was bound to the surface of NCDs (Copur et al., 2019; Kim & Kim, 2014).

Furthermore, XPS was used to analyze the surface groups and elemental compositions of NCDs and SH-NCDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, NCDs presented three significant peaks at 284, 399, and 531 eV, which corresponded to C, N, and O elements, respectively. Compared with the NCDs, there were not only C, N, and O elements in the spectra of SH-NCDs but also a new peak was observed at 164 eV, corresponding to S 2p, indicating that l-Cys bound to the surface of NCDs (Das & Mohapatra, 2017). Fig. 2D, E, and F show the high resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of NCDs. The high resolution C 1s spectrum of NCDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.3 eV, which are attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO/CN, respectively (Qin et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013). The spectrum of N 1s showed two peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.5 eV, which were assigned to CNC and NH, respectively. The O 1s spectrum indicated two peaks at 531.4 and 532.6 eV, which were associated with CO and COC/C-OH, respectively (Bao et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2015). In addition, Fig. 2C shows the S 2p spectrum decomposed into two peaks at 163.2 and 164.4 eV, which represented the SH and CS bonds, respectively (Copur et al., 2019). According to the XPS analysis, the atomic percentages of the main elements in NCDs were calculated as 67.3 % (C), 26.2 % (O), and 6.5 % (N), whereas in SH-NCDs, they were 54.24 % (C), 23.43 % (O), 16.5 % (N), and 5.84 % (S). These XPS and FT-IR results confirmed that NCDs were nitrogen-doped and SH-NCDs were synthesized from l-Cys and NCDs through an amidation reaction.

Fig. 3 A shows the optical properties of NCDs and SH-NCDs from UV–visible absorption. The NCDs had an absorption band at 330 nm, which may result from the trapping of excited state energy of the localized surface states (Dong et al., 2013). The absorption peak of SH-NCDs appeared at 260 nm. Compared with NCDs, the absorption peak of SH-NCDs had a blue shift of 70 nm, which may be due to the addition of sulfur which makes the surface energy band structure of SH-NCDs more uniform with fewer defects (Li et al., 2015). These phenomena confirmed the successful functionalization of NCDs. Fig. 3B shows that the fluorescence peak positions of NCDs appeared redshifted with the increase of excitation wavelength in the range of 300–420 nm. However, in the excitation wavelength range of 300–360 nm, the emission wavelength of SH-NCDs (Fig. 3C) did not change with the excitation wavelength, which may be due to the much more uniform surface state band structure of SH-NCDs than NCDs (Hu et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2014). As seen in Fig. 3D, the maximum excitation wavelengths of NCDs and SH-NCDs were 316 nm and 307 nm, and the maximum emission wavelengths were 417 nm and 419 nm, respectively. Compared with NCDs, the excitation wavelength of SH-NCDs was blue shifted by 9 nm, which may be attributed to the ability of the doped sulfur element to trap more excitons (Kharangarh et al., 2018). Fig. 3F shows photographs of SH-NCDs solutions under sunlight and UV light, confirming that the modified NCDs still had strong blue fluorescence. Using quinine sulfate solution (0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4, QY = 54 %) as the reference standard, the quantum yields (QY) of NCDs and SH-NCDs were calculated, which were 38.7 % and 29.6 %, respectively. Compared with NCDs, the QY of the modified SH-NCDs is reduced, which is not only due to the conversion of the strong electron-donating amino group on the CDs surface to the amide bond with a weak electron-donating property but also due to the non-radiative relaxation caused by the introduced functional group vibration (Sato et al., 2022). To investigate the stability of the SH-NCDs to light and the environment, we continuously irradiated the SH-NCDs for 3 h at the wavelength of 307 nm and recorded the fluorescence changes. As can be seen from Fig. 3G, the fluorescence intensity changed little after irradiation, indicating that the SH-NCDs had good light stability. At the same time, we observed the change of the SH-NCDs after five months of storage at room temperature, with no precipitation and floater formation, and maintained an excellent fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of photoluminescence (PL) in SH-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because the optical stability of SH-NCDs plays an important role in accurately detecting the target, the effects of pH value on its fluorescence emission have been studied. In Fig. 3H, When the pH of the solution is 1 ∼ 4, the fluorescence intensity of SH-NCDs decreases with the pH of the solution, this is because the thiol group (-SH) and thiolate salt (-S) are in equilibrium in the aqueous solution and their equilibrium ratio depends on the pH of the solution. Generally, the pKa value of the thiol group is about 8–11. When the pH value of the solution is lower than the pKa value of the thiol group, the percentage of thiolate salt in the solution decreases with the decrease of the pH value of the solution (Bayrac & Camizci, 2019). At this time, it is not favorable to the reaction of patulin with the thiol group. When the pH value was in the range of 4–11, the fluorescence intensity of SH-NCDs hardly changed significantly. When the pH value was above 11, the fluorescence intensity of SH-NCDs gradually weakened with the increase in pH.

Furthermore, the effect of salt concentration on the optical stability of SH-NCDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3I, the fluorescence response of SH-NCDs did not change significantly when the salt concentration was increased from 0 to 1.0 mol L−1, which indicated that SH-NCDs had high stability and salt tolerance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of a patulin detection method with SH-NCDs
Optimization of experimental conditions on the reaction of patulin with SH-NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the fluorescence sensor is closely related to the experimental conditions. To give full play to the performance of the fluorescence sensor, we optimized some experimental conditions, including pH value and incubation time. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when the pH range was 4–7, the fluorescence intensity increased with the pH of the solution, which was related to the coexistence and certain balance of thiol group (-SH) and thiolate (-S-) in the aqueous solution. Their equilibrium ratio depended on the pH of the solution (Drusch et al., 2007). Although patulin was relatively stable in acidic aqueous solution, it was not conducive to the binding of patulin to sulfhydryl groups under low pH conditions. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing pH in the pH range of 8–10 due to patulin’s instability in alkaline conditions. Therefore, pH 7 was the optimum pH for this experiment.

Incubation time plays an important role in the fluorescence change of the system. Therefore, we set incubation times of 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 min to evaluate the optimal incubation time. As shown in Fig. 4B, the enhancement efficiency increased with time and reached equilibrium in 15 min. When the incubation time exceeded 15 min, the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly with the increased incubation time. Ultimately, 15 min was used as the optimal reaction time.

To validate the performance of the developed sensor for patulin detection under the optimal conditions, different concentrations of patulin (0.1, 0.5,1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 ng mL−1) were prepared by diluting with 10 mmol L−1 PBS (pH = 7). As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of the probe increased with the concentration of patulin. There was a good linear relationship between the change in fluorescence signal (F/F 0) and the patulin concentration (Fig. 4D) in the range of 0.1–400 ng mL−1. The regression equation was: F/F 0 = 0.59lgC + 1.66, and the coefficient of determination (R 2) was 0.9980. The limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was 0.053 ng mL−1. These results indicated that the sensor could quantify trace amounts of patulin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the patulin detection method with SH-NCDs

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescence sensor SH-NCDs, other biotoxins that may exist in food simultaneously, including AFB1, OTA, zearalenone (ZEN), DON, patulin, and their mixtures were tested. As shown in Fig. 4E, a significant PL enhancement effect was observed after adding patulin. In contrast, other toxins have no significant effect on fluorescence intensity. A mixture of 50 ng mL−1 patulin and 200 ng mL−1 of four toxins were added to the SH-NCDs solution. Then the fluorescence response of the mix of patulin and patulin to SH-NCDs was investigated. Compared with the enhancement effect of patulin, the impact of other coexisting toxins was negligible. This showed that the proposed fluorescence sensor SH-NCDs had excellent specificity for patulin.

Different concentrations of patulin were spiked into the apple and grape juice (1, 10, and 50 ng mL−1) and the recovery ratios were evaluated, as shown in Table 1 . The recovery ratio of patulin from juice samples ranged from 88.9 % to 101.8 %, and the relative standard deviation ranged from 4.5 % to 9.2 %, indicating the potential application of the proposed sensor in detecting patulin in juice samples.

Table 2 lists the detection methods designed in this experiment and other reported detection methods for patulin. Compared with other detection methods, the fluorescence sensor SH-NCDs designed in this experiment had the advantages of simple preparation, time-saving, convenient analysis, high sensitivity, and wide linear range. This stable and inexpensive fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity to patulin and was suitable for monitoring patulin in environmental applications.

The reaction of NCDs and SH-NCDs to patulin was studied, as shown in Fig. 3E. The fluorescence in the NCDs solution barely altered before and after the addition of patulin. In contrast, the fluorescence in the SH-NCDs solution changed dramatically before and after the addition of patulin. It demonstrated that the thiol group interacted with patulin. The patulin molecule contains two conjugated unsaturated double bonds and has electrophilic properties. When the SH-NCDs exist, the sulfhydryl group on the SH-NCDs can covalently combine with C-6 in the patulin molecule, and the C-6 = C-7 double bond is transferred to the C-3 = C-7 position (Fliege & Metzler, 2000). This reaction is similar to Michael addition reaction (Fig. 1A). To explain this phenomenon, the UV spectra of SH-NCDs and SH-NCDs interacting with patulin (SH-NCDs + patulin) were compared. As shown in Fig. 3A, the UV absorption of SH-NCDs + patulin at 260 nm was significantly enhanced compared with that of SH-NCDs, which meant that a complex was formed between SH-NCDs and patulin. After adding patulin, the absorption peak of the thiol group at 2550 cm−1 (Fig. 2a) disappeared due to the covalent bond formed by the combination of patulin with the thiol group on the surface of NCDs. Compared with SH-NCDs, FT-IR of SH-NCDs with patulin added showed some shifts in the OH/NH stretching band at 3291 cm−1 and the COH stretching peak at 1282 cm−1, which further indicated that patulin and thiol groups reacted. It is well-known that patulin is a rigid planar molecule composed of two aromatic rings. The thiol group reacts with patulin to form a covalent bond, which may create a larger conjugated rigid planar structure. When patulin binds to the surface of SH-NCDs, the rigidity of SH-NCDs is enhanced so that the excited SH-NCDs reduce the energy loss caused by molecular vibration or rotation, and the exciting energy is more easily released through fluorescence radiation, thus enhancing the fluorescence of carbon dots.

In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor, SH-NCDs, for detecting patulin in fruit juices based on the Michael addition reaction between -SH and patulin was constructed. The surface of the synthetic NCDs was rich in carboxyl groups. Through an amidation reaction with l-cysteine, the sulfhydryl groups were successfully bound to the surface of the NCDs. Then, the sulfhydryl groups on the surface of the SH-NCDs reacted specifically with patulin, enhancing the fluorescence signal of the SH-NCDs. The sensor had a low detection limit of 0.053 ng mL−1 and a wide linear range of 0.1–400 ng mL−1. Compared with the other four toxins, the fluorescent probe has specific selectivity for patulin., and the recovery ratios of patulin in actual fruit juice samples were 88.9 %–101.8 %. This indicated that SH-NCDs as a fluorescent sensor for detecting patulin not only had high selectivity, low detection limit, and wide linear range but also had the advantages of low cost, simple preparation, and good stability.

Kunfeng Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yahui Guo: Visualization, Methodology. Hang Yu: Software, Validation. Yuliang Cheng: Visualization, Methodology. Yunfei Xie: Software, Validation. Weirong Yao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this article was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32172326), the National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFC1606800), the Modern agriculture in Jiangsu Province - key and general projects (BE2019362), the Science and technology planning project of Jiangsu market supervision and Administration Bureau (KJ204132, KJ21125006), the Open Project of Engineering Research Center of Dairy Quality and Safety Control Technology of the Ministry of Education of China (R202101), the Suzhou science and technology development plan (SS2019016), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680064), and the Postdoctoral Research Startup Fee of Jiangnan University (1025219032200190).
